lesson.menu_desc: Recommendation modals + Quick Fire: coach a decision -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 5 -- Giving Recommendations
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Helen Mendes | Aula 5 -- Giving Recommendations

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: A disadvantage or weak point of something
A way to solve a problem
To say something is good and worth choosing
Good enough to be worth the time, money, or effort
One of the choices you can take
To offer an idea for someone to consider
To tell someone what you think they should do

vocab.text: Notice the pair: you suggest an idea, but you recommend a clear choice. Recommend is stronger.

paras: Every week, managers face a familiar moment: two options are on the table, and someone has to choose. The first plan may be cheaper and simpler. The second may be powerful, but it comes with a higher cost. Each one has a clear benefit and a real drawback.

A good advisor does not just say "it depends." They weigh the benefit against the drawback and then make a recommendation. They might say, "I would recommend the simpler plan for now," or "You could test both before you decide." The goal is not the most impressive tool, but the solution that is genuinely worthwhile for the team today.

reading.prompt: What is the best title for this text?
choices: Why expensive tools are always better
How to recommend the option that truly fits

items: Each option in the text has both a benefit and a drawback.
The text says each one has a clear benefit and a real drawback.
A good advisor simply answers "it depends."
The text says a good advisor does NOT just say it depends.
The goal is to choose the most impressive tool.
The goal is the solution that is worthwhile for the team today, not the most impressive.

modals.title: How strong is the recommendation?
cards: Offers one possibility among others: "You could go with the cheaper plan." Followed by the base verb, no "to".
A clear, confident recommendation: "I'd recommend the cloud option," "You should test it first."
Advice with a consequence: "You'd better decide soon, or you'll lose the discount."

items: A teammate can't choose between two laptops: one is light but slow, the other is fast but heavy. Recommend one and name the benefit.
Your manager asks whether the team should buy new software now or wait six months. There is a deadline next week.
A new hire wants to know whether an expensive training course is worthwhile. Advise them, with a reason.

practice.title: Turn each cue into a recommendation. Use the modal in brackets.
items: Test the tool before you buy it.
Decide today, or you'll lose the discount.

items.situation: A colleague is choosing between a downtown office and a cheaper one far from clients. What do you recommend, and why?
tips: Weigh the main benefit against the main drawback.
Give a clear recommendation: "I'd recommend... because..."

items.situation: Your manager asks whether the team should buy new software now or wait six months. A deadline is coming.
tips: Add a warning with "You'd better..." if time is short.

items.situation: A friend can't decide between two job offers: more money or more flexibility.
tips: Name the benefit and the drawback of each.
End with a real recommendation, not just "it depends."

items.situation: A new hire wants your advice on which training course is worthwhile.
tips: Say what makes the course worth the cost.

items.situation: The team is split between two vendors and time is running out.
tips: Suggest a quick solution: "Let me suggest..."
Recommend a next step both sides can accept.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model answers
list: You could compare the two prices first.
I'd recommend testing the tool before you buy it.
You'd better decide today, or you'll lose the discount.
You should choose the simpler option.
answerkey.note: These are models, not the only correct answers. Any natural recommendation with the right modal works.

listenings.text: Hi Helen, it's James. Quick question before our meeting. We finally have two options for the new project tool. The first one is cheap and easy, but it has one big drawback: very few features. The second is powerful, yet much more expensive. I really can't decide which one is worthwhile. What would you recommend? Thanks.

This is Dana from the advisory team with a short recommendation. After comparing both vendors, my advice is clear. Vendor A offers the best price, and for a small team that benefit matters most. Vendor B is stronger, but the extra cost is not worthwhile yet. So I would recommend starting with Vendor A and reviewing the decision next year.

extra_audio.text: First, James explains that he has two options for the tool. Then Helen asks about the main benefit and drawback of each. After that, she recommends the simpler solution. Next, she advises him to test it first. Finally, James thanks her for the clear advice.
